entries.adjudication_notes: expected.json 'dead' list. README (wrapper pattern): the get_*_tools() wrappers have zero references anywhere in the project; 'it wraps a symbol that is used' is not a valid liveness argument — the wrapper itself is dead. Capped selection: expected.json lists 7 dead and 16 alive symbols; this manifest carries 3 dead + 3 alive representatives (wrapper functions, module constants, and an externally-imported accessor function) to keep the bootstrap set balanced.

finding.contract_claim: Symbol `get_file_tools` is declared but appears unused — no references were found outside its own declaration.
finding.observed_behavior: No callers or importers of `get_file_tools` were found in the fixture project; consumer modules import the get_*_tool_definitions() variants instead, so this wrapper is never invoked even though the TOOL constant it returns is alive.

entries.adjudication_notes: expected.json 'dead' list: the parameter is explicitly marked '# Backward compat:' and the only caller (run_audit in src/osoji/audit.py) never passes it, so the backward-compat plumbing that consumes it is unreachable. Capped selection: 2 of 2 dead + 2 of 3 alive params carried (junk_results omitted) to keep the bootstrap set balanced.

finding.contract_claim: Parameter `dead_code_results` is declared in the signature of build_scorecard(config, analysis_results, junk_results=None, dead_code_results=None, plumbing_result=None) (signature at lines 106-113) as an accepted input that callers may pass.
finding.observed_behavior: No call site ever passes `dead_code_results`: the only caller, run_audit in src/osoji/audit.py, invokes build_scorecard with config, analysis_results and junk_results only, so the parameter always takes its default None and the branches gated on it never execute.

entries.adjudication_notes: Known high-fanout FP pattern (no README; adjudicated from expected.json + fixture intent). expected.json pins call-site attribution: only the direct importer src/app/cli.py is a genuine call site for launcher.log — the same-named log() in src/noise.py is an unrelated function whose call sites historically polluted the evidence for common names. The parameter is read in the function body (line 2, it changes the return value), and the dead-parameter claim rests on a single attributed call site of a high-fanout common name — dismissed as the fixture's encoded FP.

finding.contract_claim: Parameter `error` is declared in the signature of log(message, error=False) (src/app/launcher.py line 1) as an accepted input that callers may pass.
finding.observed_behavior: The attributed call site, log("ok") in src/app/cli.py, does not pass `error`, so the parameter appears to always take its default False. `log` is a common name with high fan-out: src/noise.py defines an unrelated log(message) that is called locally.

entries.adjudication_notes: expected.json: one expected finding (severity error, lines 18-19, description contains 'usage'). CompletionResult has no .usage attribute — accessing it raises AttributeError at runtime.

finding.contract_claim: record_token_usage reads result.usage.get("input_tokens") and result.usage.get("output_tokens"), implying CompletionResult exposes a `usage` mapping attribute.
finding.observed_behavior: The CompletionResult dataclass defines only content, input_tokens, output_tokens, model and stop_reason — there is no `usage` attribute, so lines 18-19 raise AttributeError the first time record_token_usage is called.

entries.adjudication_notes: expected.json: expected_findings is empty. A non-null assertion (!) is an intentional developer decision, not a latent bug — the developer explicitly accepts the risk that the element exists (standard React 18 bootstrapping against index.html). This entry reconstructs the historically-proposed FP claim so Triage learns to dismiss it.

finding.contract_claim: document.getElementById("root") returns HTMLElement | null, so the non-null assertion `document.getElementById("root")!` passed to ReactDOM.createRoot will raise at runtime if the element is missing.
finding.observed_behavior: The code asserts non-null with `!` instead of a runtime check; the accompanying comment states the element is known to exist in index.html (standard React 18 bootstrapping pattern).

entries.adjudication_notes: expected.json: expected_findings is empty. The `type` discriminant check narrows the FsNode union, so accessing .children on DirectoryNode and .size/.extension on FileNode after the type guards is correct TypeScript. This entry reconstructs the historically-proposed FP claim so Triage learns to dismiss it.

finding.contract_claim: getNodeLabel accesses node.children (line 18) and node.size/node.extension (line 21) on a value typed FsNode = FileNode | DirectoryNode, and each property exists on only one union member — accessing the wrong variant would yield undefined at runtime.
finding.observed_behavior: Each access is guarded by a discriminant check on node.type (line 17): the `directory` branch reads .children and the post-guard fall-through is narrowed to FileNode before reading .size/.extension.

entries.adjudication_notes: expected.json: one expected finding (severity error, lines 8-9, description contains 'hook'). React useState after conditional returns violates the Rules of Hooks — must still be detected even after the non-null assertion and union-narrowing exemptions were added.

finding.contract_claim: React's Rules of Hooks require every hook to be called unconditionally, in the same order, on every render of a component.
finding.observed_behavior: OnboardingPage calls useState at lines 8-9 after two conditional early returns (loading at line 5, hasOrgs at line 6), so the hooks are skipped on some renders and hook state is corrupted once the conditions change between renders.

entries.adjudication_notes: expected.json: max_obligations 0, forbidden_fields include this field. These are LLM tool_use input_schema constraints that guide the model's structured output — not application configuration that code must actuate. This tools.py snapshot historically produced 8 FP obligation findings; entry reconstructs one so Triage learns to dismiss the pattern.

finding.contract_claim: The schema field `confidence` declares numeric bounds (type number, minimum 0.0, maximum 1.0) — an obligation that consuming code must enforce when the value is provided.
finding.observed_behavior: No application code reads or validates a `confidence` configuration value against these bounds; the declaration appears only inside an LLM tool_use input_schema in tools.py, where the model itself is the consumer of the constraint.

entries.adjudication_notes: expected.json: max_obligations 0, forbidden_fields include this field. These are LLM tool_use input_schema constraints that guide the model's structured output — not application configuration that code must actuate. This tools.py snapshot historically produced 8 FP obligation findings; entry reconstructs one so Triage learns to dismiss the pattern.

finding.contract_claim: The schema field `severity` declares an enum constraint (["error", "warning"]) — an obligation that consuming code must enforce when the value is provided.
finding.observed_behavior: No application code validates a `severity` configuration value against this enum; the declaration appears only inside an LLM tool_use input_schema in tools.py, where the model itself is the consumer of the constraint.

entries.adjudication_notes: No README; adjudicated from expected.json + test_plumbing_002_doc_json_fixture_stays_doc_only: docs/debugAdapterProtocol.json must remain a doc candidate and stay OUT of the schema role (the project's only schema-role file is src/runtime-schema.json), even though its symbols snapshot carries the historical file_role 'schema' misclassification. A vendored protocol-reference JSON under docs/ is documentation, not application configuration — obligations extracted from it are false positives.

finding.contract_claim: The schema definition `Request` in docs/debugAdapterProtocol.json declares a configuration structure that application code is obliged to load and actuate.
finding.observed_behavior: Nothing in the project loads docs/debugAdapterProtocol.json as runtime configuration: the file is a vendored copy of the Debug Adapter Protocol reference stored under docs/, and the project's actual runtime schema is src/runtime-schema.json.
